name: OpenTelemetry
what_it_does: OpenTelemetry is a CNCF standard for collecting distributed traces, metrics, and logs from applications. It provides language-specific SDKs (Python, Node, Go, Java, etc.), the OTLP wire protocol for telemetry export, and the OpenTelemetry Collector for routing telemetry to any backend (Jaeger, Prometheus, Datadog, Grafana, etc.).
use_cases: Instrumenting agent code to emit traces showing each reasoning step, tool call, and LLM invocation with timing
Collecting latency metrics from agent tool calls to identify bottlenecks in the reasoning loop
Propagating distributed trace context across multi-agent systems so traces show the full call graph
Exporting agent telemetry to any observability backend without vendor lock-in (Grafana, Datadog, New Relic, etc.)
Measuring LLM token usage and cost per agent run using OTel metrics
Debugging flaky agent behavior by replaying structured trace data from past runs
not_for: Teams that just need a simple hosted dashboard without any instrumentation code — OTel requires code changes
Consuming observability data — OTel is for producing telemetry, not querying it (use your backend for querying)
Non-technical teams without engineering resources to add instrumentation
Real-time alerting — OTel is the collection layer; alerting lives in the backend (Grafana, Datadog, etc.)
best_when: You are building agent infrastructure that needs vendor-neutral observability. OTel lets you instrument once and switch observability backends without re-instrumenting. Essential for production agentic systems.
avoid_when: You need a complete turnkey observability solution including storage and dashboards — OTel is only the instrumentation and collection layer. Pair it with a backend like Grafana, Jaeger, or a managed service.

auth.notes: OTel itself has no auth — it is an SDK and protocol standard. Authentication is handled by your OTLP exporter configuration pointing to a backend. Typically involves setting an API key header in the OTLP exporter config. Auth requirements depend entirely on the receiving backend.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Completely free — Apache 2.0 licensed. No limits, no cloud service required.
pricing.paid_tiers: None — OTel is entirely open source. Backend storage costs depend on chosen observability platform.
pricing.notes: OTel SDKs and Collector are free forever. The cost of observability comes from the storage backend you choose (Grafana Cloud, Datadog, etc.). Self-hosted Jaeger + Prometheus is a fully free end-to-end stack.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — it is a local SDK. Export throughput limited by network and backend ingestion capacity.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: SDK-level errors are surfaced as Python/Node/etc. exceptions. OTLP export failures are typically logged but do not crash the application by default (fail-open design). Error messages can be cryptic when OTLP endpoint is misconfigured.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Telemetry is fire-and-forget. OTel does not deduplicate spans. Duplicate spans can appear if exporters retry on network failure.
known_agent_gotchas: Trace context propagation must be explicitly configured — automatic propagation only works within a single process
LLM API calls are not auto-instrumented — you must manually create spans or use a framework like opentelemetry-instrumentation-openai
OTel Collector adds operational complexity — for simple cases, export directly to backend using OTLP/HTTP
Semantic conventions for GenAI (LLM) spans are still stabilizing as of 2024-2025 — attribute names may change
Sampling must be configured deliberately — default is no sampling (record everything), which can be expensive at scale
SDK initialization must happen before any instrumented code imports — import order matters in Python especially
OTLP/gRPC and OTLP/HTTP have different port defaults (4317 vs 4318) — easy to mix up

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS for OTLP export is strongly recommended but optional. SDK itself has no secrets. OTLP exporter credentials are typically set via environment variables. CNCF governance provides strong supply chain security for official SDK releases.
